coredata.dc:title: Renal clearing carbon dots-based near-infrared fluorescent super-small nanoprobe for renal imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Pengli
Hui, Hui
Guo, Chanjuan
Liu, Yu
Su, Ya
Huang, Xiazi
Guo, Kunxiong
Shang, Wenting
Jiang, Jingying
Tian, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Real-time renal imaging is indispensable for monitoring kidney-related disease processes and evaluating the nephrotoxicity of nanoprobes. Owing to their small size, tunable photoluminescence properties, and low biotoxicity, carbon dots (CDs) are widely employed as nanoprobes for imaging-guided disease diagnosis. However, the preparation of near-infrared CDs is a stupendous challenging. Here, CDs with near-infrared fluorescence (NIR-CDs) were prepared by one-pot pyrolysis and were comprehensively evaluated for real-time renal imaging. The NIR-CDs possessed super-small size (1.8 ± 0.2 nm) and high quantum yield (14.3% in aqueous solution) at 696 nm. NIR-CDs exhibited high-efficiency renal clearance at 24 h post-treatment (97.47–99.27% ID) via glomerular filtration. The irreversible clearance of NIR-CDs enables effective real-time monitoring of kidney function in the normal and acute kidney injury (AKI) mouse models. Consequently, NIR-CDs can be utilized for imaging analyses of impaired kidney function, pioneering a serviceable guideline for neoapplications of NIR-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared fluorescent carbon dots
Super-small size
Renal clearance
Renal imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo stability and renal clearance
In vivo fluorescence imaging of kidney dysfunction
Biosafety assessment of NIR-CDs in vivo
Comparison of NIR-CDs and established clinical detection techniques for kidney function monitoring

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of NIR-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of NIR-CDs. (c) Emission spectra of NIR-CDs with excitation wavelengths from 400 to 690 nm. (d) The NIR fluorescent lifetime decay curve with time; (e) The FT-IR spectrum; (f) The XPS; (j) TEM image, inset is the size distribution histogram of NIR-CDs; (h) HR-TEM image; (i) The SAED pattern, (g) XRD pattern and (k) Raman spectrum of NIR-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Stability and renal clearance of NIR-CDs in vivo. (a) The stability of NIR-CDs in plasma. (b) Renal clearance efficiency of NIR-CDs at the different times, each line represents a mouse, n = 3. The inset is the tangent analysis plot of the renal clearance curve for one of the mice (solid blue line). (c) Fluorescent imaging of NIR-CDs (the first row) and Cy5 (the second row) in nude mice at different times, and the images are quantitative analysis that fluorescent intensity of NIR-CDs and Cy5 in mice, respectively, n = 3. (d) Metabolic difference analysis of the NFL of NIR-CDs and Cy5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The renal clearance pathway of NIR-CDs was investigated by fluorescence imaging. (a) Schematic diagram of the renal clearance pathway of NIR-CDs. The fluorescence imaging of (b) prone position (kidney) and (c) supine position (bladder) after post-treatment with PBS, cimetidine or probenecid. (d) The quantitative analysis of the FL in prone (kidney), and error analysis was performed on the 100 min images (gray circle) (FL SBR analysis is the different groups vs. the PBS group), was no significant difference (P = 0.07 or 0.56). (e) The quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity in supine (bladder), and error analysis was performed on the 100 min images (gray circle), the FL SBR analysis was no significant difference (P = 0.09 or 0.56). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Real-time monitor of kidney function by fluorescence imaging of NIR-CDs in normal and AKI mice. The fluorescence images of (a) prone position and (b) supine position in mice treated with PBS or platinum during the different times. (c) Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity in prone (kidney), and error analysis was performed on the 100 min image (gray circle), the FL SBR analysis (the different post-treatment with the platinum group vs. the PBS group) (P = 0.02 or < 0.001). (d) Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity in supine (bladder), and error analysis was performed on the 100 min image (gray circle), the FL SBR analysis. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Biosafety experiments of NIR-CDs in vivo. (a) Body weights of mice were monitored for two weeks after treatment with PBS or NIR-CDs (25 μg/mL), respectively. (b) The photographs of hemolytic assay of NIR-CDs with different concentrations. (c) Hemolysis rates of the different groups (H2O, PBS and different concentration NIR-CDs). (d) H&E staining of major organs in various conditions (PBS or NIR-CDs) (the scale bar is 100 μm). Serum biochemical indicators (e) ALT (P
7d = 0.11, P
14d = 0.011), (f) AST (P
7d = 0.30, P
14d = 0.078), (g) ALB (P
7d = 0.19, P
14d = 0.40), (h) UREA (P
7d = 0.98, P
14d = 0.58), (i) CREA (P
7d = 0.98, P
14d = 0.39) were detected under different conditions, all serum biochemical indicators showed no significant difference compared with the PBS group. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The detection of kidney function with the clinical method. The amount of in (a) Urea (P
Pt 1d < 0.0001, P
Pt 2d < 0.0001, P
Pt 3d < 0.0001) and (b) Crea (P
Pt 1d < 0.0032, P
Pt 2d < 0.0001, P
Pt 3d < 0.0001) after post-treatment with PBS or platinum (1, 2 and 3 d). (c) The GFR of mice treated with PBS or platinum (1, 2 and 3 d) (P
Pt 1d < 0.99, P
Pt 2d < 0.52, P
Pt 3d < 0.011). (d) H&E staining of the glomerular region with treated with PBS or platinum (1, 2 and 3 d), the scale bar is 20 μm (The green arrows refer to the infiltration of immune cells in the glomerulus, and the cyan arrows refer to the dilation of the renal capsule space). (e) Immunofluorescence staining of caspase-3 expressed in the kidneys after post-treatment with platinum at the different times (the scale bar is 50 μm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic illustration of the preparation of NIR-CDs and they are applied for kidney imaging. (a) The preparation of NIR-CDs with NIR fluorescence and ultra-small size. (b) Imaging studies of NIR-CDs the renal clearance pathway and the application of NIR-Cdswere applied for imaging of kidney function.

head.ce:title: Renal clearing carbon dots-based near-infrared fluorescent super-small nanoprobe for renal imaging

Beijing, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Real-time renal imaging is indispensable for monitoring kidney-related disease processes and evaluating the nephrotoxicity of nanoprobes. Owing to their small size, tunable photoluminescence properties, and low biotoxicity, carbon dots (CDs) are widely employed as nanoprobes for imaging-guided disease diagnosis. However, the preparation of near-infrared CDs is a stupendous challenging. Here, CDs with near-infrared fluorescence (NIR-CDs) were prepared by one-pot pyrolysis and were comprehensively evaluated for real-time renal imaging. The NIR-CDs possessed super-small size (1.8 ± 0.2 nm) and high quantum yield (14.3% in aqueous solution) at 696 nm. NIR-CDs exhibited high-efficiency renal clearance at 24 h post-treatment (97.47–99.27% ID) via glomerular filtration. The irreversible clearance of NIR-CDs enables effective real-time monitoring of kidney function in the normal and acute kidney injury (AKI) mouse models. Consequently, NIR-CDs can be utilized for imaging analyses of impaired kidney function, pioneering a serviceable guideline for neoapplications of NIR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The kidney is one of the most important metabolic organs. It removes metabolites, waste products, and toxic substances [1], thereby maintaining the stability of the internal environment and metabolic processes. However, kidney injury can cause disturbances in kidney function [2], and a variety of diseases, such as chronic kidney disease, diabetes, and severe anemia, also can cause kidney dysfunction [3,4]. These diseases impose a huge economic burden on patients and have high mortality rates [5–7]. Therefore, effective real-time monitoring of kidney function is imminent. Methods commonly are used to monitor kidney function include the detection of serum urea and creatinine in the blood [8,9] as well as renal biopsy [10]; however, these methods are insensitive and invasive. Thus, it is necessary to design a noninvasive and effective method for real-time monitoring of kidney function to improve early intervention in kidney-related diseases and to evaluate the nephrotoxicity of drugs.

Fluorescence imaging benefits from its responsiveness, operability, and high sensitivity as a non-invasive and efficient technique for real-time disease monitoring [11–14]. However, studies of its application to renal function are relatively rare [15–17]. When using optical imaging to monitor renal function, the nanoprobes need to meet various requirements, including high renal clearance, small size (<6 nm), superior solubility, and low or no nephrotoxicity [18,19]. As a type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) meet these requirements [20–22]. In addition, from the perspective of the constituent elements of CDs [23–26], they are excellent in terms of biosecurity and are suitable for kidney research.

CDs, as an emerging nanomaterial, are widely applied for diagnostic imaging owing to their tunable photoluminescence, small size, high stability, excellent biocompatibility, and low nephrotoxicity [27–34]. Among these advantages, the small size (1–6 nm) of CDs is an important property for diagnosing kidney-related diseases. Some studies have demonstrated that these small-size CDs (1–6 nm) can be cleared via the kidneys [35,36]. For example, Tian et al. prepared renal-clearable Ni-CDs (∼4.6 nm) for multimodal imaging-guided therapy [35]. Su et al. developed renal-clearable Hafnium-doped CDs (3.73 ± 0.62 nm) for dual-mode imaging in hepatic carcinoma [36]. Despite clear evidence that CDs can be cleared by the kidneys, studies have not evaluated their application to assess kidney function. To this point, we prepared super-small size CDs for assessing of kidney function.

In terms of photoluminescence, tuning CDs emission into the near-infrared region has been a major challenge in diagnostic imaging of disease. The spectral bands of CDs indicated blue to red fluorescence in previous studies [37–42], limiting tissue penetration and clinical applications. Accordingly, near-infrared CDs have become a focus of recent research. Nonetheless, fewer near-infrared CDs have been reported [35,43–46]. Furthermore, reported these CDs still have certain limitations, such as the selection of inherent near-infrared precursors [43], poor water solubility [46] and low quantum yield (QY) [40,45]. In 2018, Qu and colleagues proved that the short-wavelength emission may be due to the large band gap difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CDs [47]. There are two potential approaches to reducing the band gap: 1) introducing molecules rich in CO or SO in precursors and 2) introducing molecules containing conjugated structures, resulting in large conjugated sp2 domains.

In this study, we constructed NIR-CDs with good biocompatibility, super-small size, and high QY for imaging studies of renal clearance and acute kidney injury (AKI). In particular, we used glutathione and urea as precursors to synthesize NIR-CDs by a one-pot pyrolysis method for imaging of renal clearance pathways and kidney function (Scheme 1 ). Through this method, we improved the emission wavelength of CDs and the tissue penetration ability. For the first time, we studied in detail the clearance pathway of NIR-CDs in vivo. We further demonstrated the application of NIR-CDs for kidney function imaging by constructing an AKI model. This work broadens the horizon for the biological application of NIR-CDs. The near-infrared imaging function has the potential to be applied to various diseases, including analyses of disease severity.

Glutathione (99.0%, AR, Aladdin), Urea (99%, AR, Innochem), Formamide (99.0%, AR, Aladdin), Sodium Chloride (99.0%, AR, Innochem), disodium hydrogen phosphate (99.0%, AR, Innochem) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (99.0%, AR, Innochem) were purchased from Beijing Innochem Technology Co., Ltd. Both Cell Counting Kit (CCK8) and Hoechst were purchased from Beijing Soleibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd.. Cy5 was purchased from Shaanxi Biotechnology Co., Ltd.. The heat-collecting constant temperature magnetic stirring bath was purchased from Zhengzhou Great Wall Science, Industry and Trade Co., Ltd. A vacuum freeze dryer was purchased from GOLD-SIM.

Glutathione (0.8 mM) is fully dissolve in 10 mL of formamide, adding urea (1.7 mM) to the above mixed solution. After magnetic stirring, the mixture was transferred to a round-bottom flask (25 mL), and the reaction lasted for 1 h at 180 °C. The cooled crude product was placed in dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off is 3500), and the dialysis cylinder was changed every 4 h. Finally, the sample was freeze-dried to obtain a black-green powder.

NIR-CDs solutions with the different concentrations were measured in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) to selecte the optimal imaging concentration. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the NIR-CDs solutions were measured by the UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers. Fluorescence images were acquired using the IVIS spectrum imaging system with excitation at 640 ± 10 nm and emission at 680 ± 10 nm and an acquisition time of 0.5 s.

NIR-CDs were first synthesized by one-pot pyrolysis inducing glutathione and urea at 180 °C for 1 h (Fig. 1 a), and the yield of NIR-CDs obtained by this method was 12.29%. Glutathione was chosen as the carbon source and urea was employed as the passivator to ameliorate the luminous efficiency of NIR-CDs. The mixed doping reaction of the precursors introduces a large rigid plane, conferred near-infrared regional luminescence [49]. We verified the optical properties of NIR-CDs by various detection techniques. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, NIR-CDs possessed three UV–Vis absorption peaks located at 421 nm, 641 nm, and 692 nm (black line); the first peak could be belonged to transition of the aromatic CC, and the latter two peaks may be attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 CO, CN, and CS [48,49]. NIR-CDs had excitation-independent fluorescent property, and 696 nm was the optimal emission under excitation at 420 nm (Fig. 1c). Therefore, NIR-CDs exhibit near-infrared fluorescence. The near-infrared fluorescent QY of NIR-CDs was 14.3%. Compared with the QYs of previously reported red or near-infrared CDs in aqueous solution (Table S1), the NIR-CDs had excellent solubility and high near-infrared QY. The fluorescence lifetime with mono-exponential decay (Fig. 1d) of NIR-CDs was 4.73 ns. These optical analyses clearly demonstrated that NIR-CDs were successfully prepared and showed predominant solubility and high QY in the near-infrared region.

We investigated the composition and structure of NIR-CDs. Determination of functional groups on the NIR-CDs surface by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1e, there were two broad absorption peaks located at 3400 and 3220 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H, respectively. The remaining four spikes (1684, 1605, 1393, and 1234 cm−1) belonged to CO, -CC–CC-/CN, C–N, and C–O bonds, respectively. The peak was located at 672 cm−1 (fingerprint region), belonged to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the -C-H bond. We further demonstrated the elemental and structural composition of NIR-CDs based on X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The full XPS (Fig. 1f) proved that NIR-CDs were composed of the elements C (1s, 284.79 eV, 56.78%), O (1s, 530.64 eV, 22.09%), N (1s, 399.29 eV, 20.54%), S (2p, 162.3 eV, 0.59%), and H. As shown in Fig. S1a, the high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) O 1s of NIR-CDs was decomposed into two binding energies at 530.5 and 531.8 eV, which correspond to C–O/C–OH and CO bond, respectively. The HR-XPS of N 1s (Fig. S1b) displayed the presence of pyridinic N (397.6 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.3 eV). The binding energy of C 1s (Fig. S1c) was decomposed into six peaks at 283.7, 284.5, 285.0, 285.4, 286.0, and 287.1 eV, assigning to aromatic carbon, aliphatic carbon, C–S, C–N, C–O, and CO/N–CO/O–CO, respectively. In the HR-XPS of S 2p (Fig. S1d), the binding energy was fitted to six peaks (160.7, 161.6, 162.7, 163.4, 164.1, and 167.3 eV), belonging to thiophene S (2p3/2 and 2p1/2, 163.4 and 164.1 eV), sulfate (167.3 eV), thiolate (162.7 eV), and = C-SOx (x = 2, 3, 4). Accordingly, NIR-CDs included five component elements (C, N, O, S, and H) and substantial functional groups. And the zeta potential of NIR-CDs is −23.68 mV (Fig. S2). This is due to the large number of negatively charged functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and sulfonic acid groups) on the surface of NIR-CDs from the result of XPS and FT-IR spectra.

The morphology and size of NIR-CDs were measured via Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1j displays the TEM image of NIR-CDs revealing uniform monodisperse nanoparticles, with an average size of 1.8 ± 0.3 nm, as determined using ImageJ. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1h) displayed sharp lattice fringes with an d-spacing of 0.21 nm, belonging to graphene (100) lattice fringes [42]. Fig. 1i shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) results, which revealed that NIR-CDs possess high graphene crystallinity. The NIR-CDs (Fig. 1g) had a broad peak centered at 26.9° in the XRD pattern, demonstrating a disordered structure [32,50]. The carbonization degree of the carbon core was investigated by Raman spectra (Fig. 1k). We observed two characteristic peaks located at 1349 nm and 1504 nm. The ID/IG value was 0.89, confirming that the structure of NIR-CDs contained more aromatic domains and greater graphitization [50].

To enable applications of the nanoprobe in biology, we verified its optical stability. When the temperature reached 65 °C (Fig. S3a), the near-infrared fluorescence decreased slightly, proving NIR-CDs have good thermal stability. In the presence of UV light (365 nm), the fluorescent intensity of NIR-CDs remained basically unchanged after 4 h (Fig. S3b), revealing that NIR-CDs have photobleaching resistance. In salt solution with different concentrations, the intensity of NIR-CDs remained basically unchanged (Fig. S3c), showing that NIR-CDs have excellent stability in salt solution. In addition, we detected changes in the intensity of NIR-CDs at different pH values (Fig. S3d). Relatively stable optical properties were maintained and the position of emission peak remained the same. After the storage of NIR-CDs at 20 °C for 60 d (Fig. S3e), the changes in the optimal emission peaks with respect to intensity and position were negligible. At the same time, the colloidal stability of NIR-CDs in water, PBS and DMEM within a week was monitored by means of fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence intensity of NIR-CDs fluctuated slightly in these three solutions, and the changes basically can be neglected (Fig. S3f), demonstrating NIR-CDs have good colloidal stability in different media solutions. The observed stability shows that NIR-CDs can be effectively applied in organismal research.

The effect of NIR-CDs on HUVEC cells proliferation was assessed by the CCK8 assay [43]. When the concentration of NIR-CDs was 0.6 mg/mL, cell viability was approximately 98% (Fig. S4), indicating high biosafety for biological applications. Using different cell lines (human tongue cancer cells, pancreatic cells, and macrophages cells) to further verify the imaging ability of NIR-CDs by turntable confocal microscopy (Fig. S5). After incubation with NIR-CDs with various concentrations, we observed efficient cellular uptake and good fluorescent imaging characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo stability and renal clearance
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the results of in vitro analyses, we further utilized NIR-CDs for in vivo imaging. Imaging of NIR-CDs at different concentrations was performed using an intravital imager, and the optimal excitation/emission wavelengths (Ex: 640 nm, Em: 680 nm) and imaging concentration (25 μg/mL) were selected Fig. S6). Previous studies have shown that nanoprobes of <6 nm can be metabolized by the glomerulus. Therefore, our analyses focused on the imaging of kidney function in vivo.

In addition to stability in urine (Fig. S7), we investigated the blood stability of NIR-CDs in vivo. We injected NIR-CDs into mice through the tail vein, collected the plasma containing NIR-CDs, and evaluated the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2 a). As shown in Fig. 2a, we observed a 3 nm blue-shift in the peak of NIR-CDs, which is basically negligible, proving that NIR-CDs have good stability in both blood and urine and the optimal emission peak is not affected by the biological environment in vivo.

To study renal clearance, NIR-CDs were detected in the urine of mice. The offline urinalysis method was used for evaluating renal clearance efficiency. Urine samples were collected from normal mice, followed by NIR-CDs incubation and optical measurement. In the offline urinalysis (Fig. S7a and b), the NIR-CDs with different concentrations in urine possessed a strong linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9985), and the absorption peaks of NIR-CDs did not differ substantially among concentrations. These results indicate that NIR-CDs have a strong linear relationship and stability in urine. Based on the observed linear relationship, the renal clearance efficiency of NIR-CDs was approximately 97.47–99.27% ID at 24 h (Fig. 2b). We further analyzed the clearance curve in mice by determining the intersection of tangents at the inflection point of the curve (solid blue line). As shown in the inset of Fig. 2b, the intersection of the two tangent lines was located at (3.24163, 97.84036), indicating that NIR-CDs achieve 97.84% clearance within 3.24 h. After 24 h, images of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidneys, and bladder) were obtained, and no fluorescent signal was observed (Fig. S7c). These results show that NIR-CDs can nearly achieve complete clearance at 24 h post-injection. The high renal clearance efficiency of NIR-CDs could be attributed to their high hydrophilicity and small size (1.8 ± 0.3 nm) relative to the glomerular filtration size cutoff (<6 nm) [51].

We further investigated the renal clearance ability of NIR-CDs compared to that of the small molecule Cy5. As shown in Fig. 2c, NIR-CDs were almost completely metabolized within 24 h. The content of Cy5 in vivo was much higher than that of NIR-CDs at 24 h. These results were supported by a semi-quantitative analysis of fluorescent signal. NIR-CDs were metabolized faster than Cy5 in vivo (Fig. 2d). Within 24 h of imaging, the difference in the normalized fluorescence intensity (NFL) indicating the metabolized fraction of NIR-CDs was ΔNFL = 0.84, while that of Cy5 was ΔNFL = 0.07. These data explicitly showed that NIR-CDs were completely metabolized within 24 h, while Cy5 metabolism began at a slow rate after 24 h. These results demonstrated that NIR-CDs have faster renal clearance compared to that of Cy5.

The two major metabolic pathways in vivo include the monocyte-macrophage system and renal clearance [18]. Renal clearance pathways include glomerular filtration, tubular secretion, and reabsorption (Fig. 3 a) [16,52]. Glomerular filtration is the paramount line of defense for renal clearance, and nanoprobes filtering by the glomerulus requires a size of less than 6 nm [19]. Renal tubular secretion and reabsorption are both mediated by proteins in renal tubular cells. Probenecid [53] and cimetidine [54] have been used to block these proteins for secretion and reabsorption, respectively. To study the renal clearance pathway for NIR-CDs in detail, we evaluated the kidneys and bladder of mice by optical imaging. Healthy mice were randomized into three groups, treated with PBS (200 μL), probenecid (150 mg/mL, 200 μL), and cimetidine (150 mg/mL, 200 μL) for 0.5 h, followed by injection with NIR-CDs via the tail vein. Images of mice in the prone and supine positions were obtained at different times (from pre-treatment to 100 min). NIR-CDs rapidly accumulated in the kidney at 5 min post-treatment in the PBS group (Fig. 3b), and the fluorescence signal in the kidneys became weaker over time. In the supine position (Fig. 3c), after 10 min in the PBS group, the fluorescence signal in the bladder was the strongest and gradually weakened with time. Macroscopically, images of the PBS group indicated that NIR-CDs were rapidly cleared through the renal metabolic pathway. In addition, we observed the same trend in the probenecid and cimetidine-treated groups (Fig. 3d and e). Post-treatment with probenecid and cimetidine, images of mice in the prone position revealed that NIR-CDs accumulation in the kidney was greatest at 5 min, and images in the supine position showed the same results as the PBS group.

We analyzed images of the kidney and bladder separately over 100 min (magnified area shown by the gray dashed line in Fig. 3d and e). In the kidney (100 min), the fluorescent signal-to-background ratio (FL SBR) of the cimetidine-treated group was 1.10-fold higher than that of the PBS group, and this difference was not significant (P = 0.08). The FL SBR in the probenecid-treated group was 0.95-fold than that of the PBS group, and there was also no manifest difference (P = 0.56). In the bladder (100 min), the FL SBR of the cimetidine group was 1.06-fold higher than that of the PBS group, which was not obviously different (P = 0.09). The FL SBR in the probenecid-treated group was 0.99-fold that of the PBS group, it was also no significant difference (P = 0.56). These results proved that the clearance pathway of NIR-CDs involves glomerular filtration alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging of kidney dysfunction
ce:section.ce:para: We further constructed an AKI mouse model to investigate the renal clearance function of NIR-CDs. Since the clearance pathway of NIR-CDs is limited to glomerular filtration, without renal tubular secretion or reabsorption, and the fluorescent signal changes of NIR-CDs in the kidneys and bladder are employed for assessing whether renal function is impaired. Therefore, we believe that the NIR-CDs can be applied as an optical nanoprobe for monitoring of kidney function. We successfully constructed AKI mice model for imaging of the kidney and bladder at various times (1 d, 2 d, and 3 d post-treatment with platinum), using PBS-treated mice as the control group.

We constructed mice models of AKI with varying degrees of damage for imaging in prone and supine positions (Fig. 4 a and b). When the post-treatment time did not exceed 1 d, the fluorescence signals in the kidney and bladder showed the same trend as that observed in the PBS group. The NIR-CDs signal was strongest at 5 min in the kidney, and the signal in the bladder was strongest at 10 min. When the platinum treatment time exceeded 1 d, we observed that the signal in the kidney began to decline compared with that the PBS group, and the effect in the kidney was clearly observed. The bladder signals showed that NIR-CDs were slowly excreted from the kidneys to the bladder over time. However, when the mice were treated for 3 d, the signal retention time of NIR-CDs in the kidney was prolonged, and the corresponding signal in the bladder increased gradually. These results can also be intuitively revealed by a fluorescence quantitative line chart ( Fig. 4c and d). The above data show that kidney injury can be effectively monitored by real-time fluorescent imaging.

We analyzed images of the kidney and bladder separately at 100 min in different post-treatment groups (magnified area indicated by the gray dashed line in Fig. 4c and d). In the kidney (100 min), the FL SBR of the platinum-treated group at 1 d was 1.07-fold higher than that of the PBS group, the difference was significant (P = 0.03). The FL SBR values in the platinum-treated group at 2 d and 3 d were 1.19-fold and 1.69-fold higher than that of the PBS group, respectively, and these differences were significant (P < 0.001); In the bladder (100 min), the FL SBR value in the platinum-treated group at 1 d, 2 d and 3 d was 1.31-fold, 1.84-fold and 1.69-fold higher than that of the PBS group, respectively. The FL SBR values in the platinum-treated group (1–3 d) were significantly higher than that of the PBS group (P < 0.001). We selected 5 min and 100 min after injection of NIR-CDs in the PBS group and the AKI group to obtain their main organs, and the main organs of different groups were subjected to ex vivo fluorescence imaging and fluorescence quantification of kidney and bladder was performed (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. S8a-c, we observed that NIR-CDs were mainly distributed in the kidney and bladder at 5 min, and the fluorescence signal in the kidney and bladder weakened with time extending to 100 min. In the AKI mice (Fig. S8d-f), the fluorescence signal of kidneys was higher than that of the PBS group at the same time point, and the fluorescence signal of bladder was gradually enhanced, which suggests that NIR-CDs can be employed for renal function imaging. We further performed statistical difference analysis on the kidneys and bladder between the PBS group and the AKI group, respectively (Fig. S8g-h). At different imaging times, the fluorescence signal of kidneys and bladder of the AKI mice were compared with those of the PBS group, there are significant differences. Therefore, it is more clearly demonstrated that NIR-CDs can be used for renal function imaging through ex vivo imaging and quantitative analysis. These results demonstrated that NIR-CDs are effective for imaging analyses of kidney dysfunction. This is because prolonged platinum induction results in severe glomerular lesions, which significantly reduce kidney function and the glomerular filtration rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety assessment of NIR-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility is a vital parameter to evaluate nanoprobes for in vivo biomedical applications. In an analysis of body weight, we found that NIR-CDs had no effect on the growth of mice over 2 weeks (Fig. 5 a), revealing that NIR-CDs are not toxic to mice. As summarized in Fig. 5b and c, a hemolytic test was carried out to determine the hemocompatibility of NIR-CDs. No distinct hemolysis of red blood cells was observed in PBS and NIR-CDs solution at a concentration of up to 100 μg/mL, while most red blood cells underwent hemolysis in water. By UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, the hemolysis rates of NIR-CDs were quantitatively evaluated. The hemolysis rates for all tested concentrations of NIR-CDs in PBS containing red blood cells unexceeded 3.19%. Such values are far below the critical safe hemolysis rate specified in ISO/TR7406 [55]. These results established the high hemocompatibility of NIR-CDs, supporting their value for in vivo research.

We further verified the safety of NIR-CDs with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. We randomly divided mice into three groups: PBS, NIR-CDs (7 d), and NIR-CDs (14 d). The H&E staining results for the main organs in pathological sections were similar in the three groups (Fig. 5d), proving that NIR-CDs have good biosafety. We further assessed the safety of NIR-CDs with respect to liver and kidney function at different times. We analyzed the significant difference analysis of NIR-CDs on liver and kidney function at different times. Fig. 5e–i displays the results of serum biochemical analyses on the 7th and 14th. We found that there was no significant difference (ALT P
7d = 0.11, P
14d = 0.011, AST P
7d = 0.30, P
14d = 0.078, ALB P
7d = 0.19, P
14d = 0.40, UREA P
7d = 0.98, P
14d = 0.58, CREA P
7d = 0.98, P
14d = 0.39) in all serum biochemical indicators compared with the PBS group on the 7th and 14th after NIR-CDs injection, respectively. All parameters fluctuated within normal ranges. These results collectively supported the excellent biosafety of NIR-CDs and provide a foundation for future clinical transformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of NIR-CDs and established clinical detection techniques for kidney function monitoring
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate thatNIR-CDs were applied for the detection of kidney dysfunction, Urea and Crea in the blood as well as the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) were detected by clinical detection techniques in the PBS and AKI groups. The significant increases in Urea and Crea after 3 d platinum-treatment (Fig. 6 a and b), and the contents of Urea (9-fold) and Crea (6-fold) were far higher than that of the PBS group. Meanwhile, we also analyzed the differences in the contents of Urea and Crea between the PBS group and the platinum-treatment AKI group at different times. Compared with the PBS group, the Urea content after platinum-treatment for 1 d, 2 d and 3 d was significantly different (P
Pt 1d < 0.0001, P
Pt 2d < 0.0001, P
Pt 3d < 0.0001). And the Crea content in the AKI groups was also significantly different from that of the PBS group (P
Pt 1d < 0.0032, P
Pt 2d < 0.0001, P
Pt 3d < 0.0001). Accordingly, we successfully constructed the AKI model, leading to renal insufficiency. Then, we further examined the imbalance in renal function by GFR (Fig. 6c) [56]. The GFR is 10.1 μL min−1 g−1 in the PBS group. After treatment with platinum (3 d), the GFR of FITC-inulin was reduced to 4.7 μL min−1 g−1, which was 0.46-fold that of the PBS group. We conducted the significant difference analysis of GFR and found that after platinum-treatment 1 d and 2 d, these was no different from that of the PBS group (P
Pt 1d < 0.99, P
Pt 2d < 0.52). However, after treatment with platinum (3 d), the GFR of the AKI mice was significantly different from that of the PBS group (P
Pt 3d < 0.011).

Next, we investigated the platinum-induced kidney changes at the tissue level based on pathological sections. As shown Fig. 6d, H&E staining revealed that the morphology of the glomerulus was normal up to 1 d after platinum induction, in which there were few infiltrating immune cells. However, when the induction time was prolonged, we found that the kidney capsule space progressively thickened and the glomerulus showed immune cell infiltration. When the induction time was 3 d, some glomeruli were lysed and others showed substantial immune cells infiltration. The expression of caspase-3 in the kidney after platinum induction was verified by an immunofluorescence staining experiment (Fig. 6e). The expression of caspase-3 was significantly increased at 3 d post-treatment with platinum. These results were consistent with the images data after treatment with platinum, suggesting that NIR-CDs are suitable for real-time monitoring of kidney function by fluorescent imaging (Fig. 4). The above typical clinical detection techniques are invasive, time-consuming, and not applicable for real-time imaging; while fluorescence imaging by NIR-CDs is an effective approach for real-time, rapid, visual monitoring of kidney function.

The near-infrared luminescence of CDs is an important area of recent research. However, their short-wavelength emission limits tissue penetration, resulting in poor imaging and restricting the biological applications. Owing to their small size, tunable photoluminescence properties, and low toxicity, CDs can be employed for imaging studies of kidney function. In this study, we introduced carbonyl-containing urea to synthesize near-infrared fluorescent NIR-CDs. Fluorescence spectroscopy, TEM, and CCK8 assays showed that the prepared NIR-CDs possessed near-infrared fluorescence (696 nm), ultra-small size (1.8 ± 0.3 nm), and low toxicity (Fig. 1). Accordingly, the NIR-CDs are effective optical nanoprobes for monitoring kidney function.

Nanoprobes with good stability are essential for biological applications. The prepared NIR-CDs have excellent stability under different conditions (Fig. S2) and wonderful optical stability in serum (Fig. 2a). The renal clearance capacity of nanoprobes is critical for analyses of kidney function. In urinalysis, the renal clearance rate of NIR-CDs was up to 97.84% (Fig. 2b). Compared with those of Cy5, the NIR-CDs have faster renal clearance rate (Fig. 2c and d), which could be attributed to the high hydrophilicity and small size of NIR-CDs. Therefore, NIR-CDs have various advantages for monitoring.

Fluorescence imaging provides a non-invasive, real-time, and responsive imaging technique for monitoring kidney function. The kidney is one of the largest clearing organs, and renal clearance mainly occurs via three pathways: glomerular filtration, tubular secretion, and reabsorption. As determined using blocking agents, the NIR-CDs were cleared by glomerular filtration (Fig. 3), further verifying their feasibility for the assessment of kidney function (metabolic excretion). As the degree of kidney injury increased, the retention time of NIR-CDs in the kidney was prolonged and the fluorescence signal in the bladder increased over time (Fig. 4). Thus, NIR-CDs can be used to monitor impaired kidney function. Overall, in vivo imaging studies supported the feasibility of the NIR-CDs for real-time monitoring of kidney function.

As an exogenous substance, the safety of nanoprobes must be demonstrated for in vivo applications. Based on analyses of mouse body weight, hemolysis rate, H&E staining (major organs), and serum biochemical indicators (Fig. 5), NIR-CDs had excellent biosafety. We further compared fluorescence imaging using NIR-CDs and common clinical detection methods. The expression levels of kidney function indicators were tested by blood sampling and the GFR was assessed by FITC-inulin, H&E, and immunofluorescence staining (Fig. 6). These clinical detection methods demonstrated that as the model induction time increased, the severity of kidney injury increased. However, these clinical methods have various drawbacks, such as invasive and time-consuming processes and low stability. NIR-CDs are advantageous for the real-time monitoring of the kidney and bladder by fluorescence imaging by enabling real-time, non-invasive, and rapid detection. We clearly demonstrated that NIR-CDs with near-infrared fluorescence, ultra-small size, low nephrotoxicity, and high renal clearance can be applied for real-time monitoring of kidney function.

To sum up the above, we prepared a near-infrared regional NIR-CDs-based fluorescent nanoprobe for kidney imaging. NIR-CDs exhibited near-infrared fluorescence, ultra-small size, negligible nephrotoxicity, high renal clearance efficiency via glomerular filtration alone, enabling the real-time monitor of kidney function in vivo. With the aid of the fluorescence imaging, real-time monitoring of normal and abnormal kidney function is achieved. NIR-CDs had various advantages over current clinical methods for analyses of kidney function, including real-time, non-invasive, and rapid monitoring ability. Overall, the NIR-CDs are expected to expand the application of CDs and are a candidate nanoplatform for real-time imaging of kidney function-related disease processes. With further development, this nanoprobe may provide a promising tool for non-invasive diagnosis during disease development.

Pengli Gao: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Hui Hui: Project administration, Formal analysis. Chanjuan Guo: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yu Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ya Su: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiazi Huang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Kunxiong Guo: Data curation, Formal analysis. Wenting Shang: Supervision, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jingying Jiang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jie Tian: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Thanks to Professor Su Kun and all the laboratory platforms for supporting the experiments in the article. We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing. We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) and Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co., Ltd. (www.zkbaice.cn) for supporting some characterization results.
